title: Intervention Letter
entryBody: A guided three-paragraph structure — taken directly from Matt's intervention handout. Brevity is critical. One side of a single page.
sendErrorBody: Your letter is still here. Check your connection and try sending it again.

p1.title: Why I'm Here
p1.coachNote: Paragraph 1: Tell them why you're here. Share what you love about them and why you want them in your life when they're healthy. If you have a memory where you felt close to them or proud of them, share it — let them hear who they are beneath the addiction.
p1.prompt: What do you love about them, and what does your relationship look like when they're healthy?
p1.placeholder: I'm here because I love you. I remember when you… That's the person I know you are.
p1.memoryPrompt: Include a shared memory (optional but powerful)
p1.memoryPlaceholder: A moment you felt close, a time you were proud of them, or the relationship at its best.

p2.title: How This Has Affected Me
p2.coachNote: Paragraph 2: Don't list bad choices or behaviors — those can be disputed. Instead, share 2–3 specific experiences as the vehicle for your feelings. Focus on what you felt, not what they did wrong. The format is: "When you _____, I felt _____."
p2.openerLabel: Opening sentence (edit to fit your relationship)
p2.maxReached: 3 experiences maximum — in the room, fewer words carry more weight.
p2.hints: "When you came home that night, I felt terrified." · "When I got that call, I felt completely alone." · "When I watched our kids' faces, I felt helpless."
p2.toneFlag: Tone check: describe one specific moment and how it made you feel — avoid blame or absolutes.

p3.title: The Request & The Boundary
p3.coachNote: Paragraph 3: Ask them to go to treatment today — name who will go with them, and emphasize today. Delaying almost always leads to a worse outcome. Express hope for what the relationship can become if they get sober. Then name what must change if they don't go — not as punishment, but to stop enabling or being harmed. Ask yourself: Have I enabled this? Have I been harmed? If yes to either, name how your behavior must change. End with love.
p3.requestLabel: I want you to go to treatment today
p3.requestNote: Name who will go with them. Emphasize today — not tomorrow, not next week.
p3.hopeLabel: Hope for the relationship if they go and stay sober
p3.hopePlaceholder: I hope we can rebuild trust. I hope I can stop worrying. I want to make new memories with you.
p3.supportPlaceholder: I'll go to family sessions with you. I'll spend time with you when you're sober. I'll be proud of you.
p3.boundariesEyebrow: If they choose not to go today
p3.boundariesNote: Have you enabled this addiction or been harmed by it — financially, emotionally, or physically? If yes to either, name how your behavior must change. This may mean stepping back from the relationship, withdrawing money, housing, or a phone — or simply no longer staying silent. Only include what you will genuinely follow through on.
p3.boundaryConfirmLabel: I will follow through on this.
p3.noBoundaries: Add your boundaries in the Boundaries tab first, then return here.
p3.closingNote: End where you started — with love and an open invitation to accept help today.

preview.title: Your letter
preview.overflowWarning: Your letter must fit on one side of a single page. In the room, shorter is stronger.
preview.referralCard: A letter like this is most powerful read with professional support in the room.

defaults.openerLabel: Your substance use has affected me in the following ways.
defaults.request: I want you to choose to go to treatment today.
defaults.closing: Do you see that you need help, and are you willing to accept it today?
